name: Ancient Greek Alphabet
approx_period: c. 800 BCE – 300 BCE (archaic/classical epigraphy); variant forms persist through Byzantine period
extant_corpus_size: ~150,000+ inscriptions in the Inscriptiones Graecae (IG) and Supplementum Epigraphicum Graecum (SEG) series
key_source_documents: Inscriptiones Graecae (IG), Berlin-Brandenburg Academy of Sciences, 1873–present — primary epigraphic corpus
Supplementum Epigraphicum Graecum (SEG), Brill, 1923–present — annual supplement
Dipylon Inscription (oenochoë, Athens), c. 740 BCE — one of the earliest datable Greek alphabetic inscriptions
Rosetta Stone (hieroglyphic / Demotic / Greek), 196 BCE — key trilingual anchor for comparative decipherment
visual_characteristics: Archaic Greek inscriptions show regional variation (boustrophedon writing — alternating left-to-right and right-to-left — is common before c. 500 BCE) with letters mirrored on right-to-left lines. Classical Attic epigraphy standardises to left-to-right stoichedon grids (letters aligned in even columns). Letters are angular, with sigma written as a three-bar form (Ϲ becomes S-form later); epsilon and omicron appear unaccented. The Ionic/Classical alphabet of 24 letters (from c. 403 BCE Athenian decree) is the canonical epigraphic standard.
notes: Episode T3_dryrun references this system. Descendant of the Phoenician abjad; ancestor of the Latin, Cyrillic, and Coptic alphabets.
